# Minimally Invasive Thoracic Surgery

Generously supported by Intuitive.
Special thanks to Hackensack Meridian Health for the use of their facilities and assistance with this video.

## What Is Minimally Invasive Surgery?

Minimally invasive thoracic surgery is a way of performing surgery in the chest through small incisions, without making large cuts or incisions in the body, and does not require spreading apart of the ribs. Surgeons use a camera and instruments to get to the lung through small incisions in between the ribs. There are two different options available for minimally invasive thoracic surgery; video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) and robotic-assisted surgery.

[**Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS)**](/lung-health-diseases/lung-procedures-and-tests/video-assisted-thoracic-surgery) is a procedure in which a small tube called a thoracoscope is inserted through a small incision between the ribs. At the end of the tube is a small camera. This lets the surgeon see the entire chest cavity without having to open up the chest or spread the ribs. The surgeon then removes lung tissue with specially designed instruments inserted through one or two additional small incisions.

In [**robotic-assisted surgery**](/lung-health-diseases/lung-procedures-and-tests/robotic-thoracic-surgery)**,** a surgeon will sit at a console next to the patient in the operating room and control the instruments, including a camera, on the robotic surgical system. A small 3D high definition camera is placed through one of the small incisions to provide a good view of the inside of the chest cavity, while wristed robotic instruments are inserted through the other small incisions made in between the ribs. The surgeon removes lung tissue through one of the small incisions. The use of the wristed instruments lets the surgeon perform the surgery without having to make larger incisions to open up the chest or spread the ribs.

## What to Expect

## What Are the Risks and Benefits?

Like all surgery, there are risks to minimally invasive thoracic surgery. They include infection, bleeding, abnormal heartbeats and air leakage from your lungs and pain. Robotic-assisted surgery may be associated with longer operative times and a longer time under anesthesia. Your surgeon may have to convert to a more invasive approach if needed to complete the surgery.

However, there are benefits to minimally invasive surgery compared to open chest surgery through a [thoracotomy](/lung-health-diseases/lung-procedures-and-tests/thoracotomy), depending on which minimally invasive approach you receive you may experience:

Each person handles surgery a little differently and your outcomes may depend on a number of factors, like your health history, the type of lung cancer you have and/or your surgeon's experience.

## Who Is Eligible?

Surgeons evaluate whether each patient is a candidate for minimally invasive thoracic surgery on a case by case basis. Surgeons consider the patient's specific condition, medical history and anatomy when deciding if they are eligible for minimally invasive surgery. Talk to your doctor about whether this type of surgery is right for you.

*Page last updated: November 20, 2024*

# Minimally Invasive Surgery

## OVERVIEW

There are a variety of diseases and conditions of the colon and rectum for which surgery is recommended.  In addition to the decision to undergo surgery, patients are often faced with a choice of traditional or minimally invasive surgical techniques.  In order to participate fully in the decision-making process, patients need information about the following issues:

Minimally invasive colon and rectal surgery is a continually changing field.  Every year brings new information based on further refinements and increasing use of these techniques.  The information and opinions in this material are based on the current state of the art in 2024.

## HOW ARE TRADITIONAL OPEN COLON AND RECTAL OPERATIONS PERFORMED?

In order to perform operations inside the abdomen, surgeons must make an incision large enough to offer adequate visibility, provide access to the abdominal organs and allow the use of hand-held surgical instruments.  These incisions may be placed in different parts of the abdominal wall.  Depending on the size of the patient and the type of operation, the incision may be 6 to 12 inches in length.  There is a significant amount of discomfort associated with these incisions that can prolong the time spent in the hospital after surgery and can limit how quickly a patient can resume normal daily activities.  Because traditional techniques have long been used and taught to generations of surgeons, they are widely available and are considered the standard treatment to which newer techniques must be compared.

## WHAT IS MINIMALLY INVASIVE SURGERY?

Minimally invasive procedures use advanced technologies to avoid the need for the large incisions used in traditional open surgery.  The development of these techniques has been an important advance for the benefit of patients and is useful for the treatment of many conditions.  Minimally invasive surgery can include laparoscopic surgery, robotic surgery, or even newer endoluminal surgical techniques.   
Minimally invasive surgical approaches have been developed to perform operations through smaller incisions, reducing the discomfort and healing complications associated with long incisions.  Minimally invasive surgery can be used to effectively treat a variety of common benign and malignant colon and rectal conditions. All minimally invasive techniques require advanced surgical skills and specialized equipment.  Many surgeons have had advanced training in minimally invasive surgery and can discuss the specific techniques they recommended for surgery.  All minimally invasive surgeries are performed with the patient asleep under a general anesthetic.  All the techniques listed below are considered “minimally invasive,” but may vary slightly in their overall advantages and disadvantages.

## HOW IS MINIMALLY INVASIVE SURGERY PERFORMED?

## BENEFITS OF MINIMALLY INVASIVE SURGERY

Incisions are much smaller than those used in traditional surgery. Potential benefits include:

## IS MINIMALLY INVASIVE SURGERY SAFE?

## WHEN IS MINIMALLY INVASIVE SURGERY AN OPTION?

## QUESTIONS FOR YOUR SURGEON:

## WHAT IS A COLON AND RECTAL SURGEON?

Colon and rectal surgeons are experts in the surgical and non-surgical treatment of diseases of the colon, rectum and anus. They have completed advanced surgical training in the treatment of these diseases as well as full general surgical training. Board certified colon and rectal surgeons complete residencies in general surgery and colon and rectal surgery, and pass intensive examinations conducted by the American Board of Surgery and the American Board of Colon and Rectal Surgery. They are well-versed in the treatment of both benign and malignant diseases of the colon, rectum and anus and are able to perform routine screening examinations and surgically treat conditions if indicated to do so.

## DISCLAIMER

The American Society of Colon and Rectal Surgeons is dedicated to ensuring high-quality patient care by advancing the science, prevention and management of disorders and diseases of the colon, rectum and anus. These brochures are inclusive but not prescriptive. Their purpose is to provide information on diseases and processes, rather than dictate a specific form of treatment. They are intended for the use of all practitioners, health care workers and patients who desire information about the management of the conditions addressed. It should be recognized that these brochures should not be deemed inclusive of all proper methods of care or exclusive of methods of care reasonably directed to obtain the same results. The ultimate judgment regarding the propriety of any specific procedure must be made by the physician in light of all the circumstances presented by the individual patient.

# Risks of Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery

Our Approach to Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery

Minimally invasive spine surgery (MISS) is a distinct surgical approach that combines a unique philosophy of care with advanced surgical techniques. Because it uses small incisions and state-of-the-art techniques and technology, MISS is highly effective and less damaging to surrounding tissues than traditional surgery. Patients typically experience less pain, heal faster, and have fewer complications.

Our skilled surgeons offer MISS for a wide range of spine conditions. We personalize care to your needs and preferences to achieve the best possible results.

## What We Offer You for Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery

#### INTERESTED IN AN ONLINE SECOND OPINION?

The Stanford Medicine Online Second Opinion program offers you easy access to our world-class doctors. It’s all done remotely, and you don’t have to visit our hospital or one of our clinics for this service. You don’t even need to leave home!

Visit our **[online second opinion](https://stanfordhealthcare.org/second-opinion/overview.html)** page to learn more.

## What to Expect During Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery

MISS involves using specialized tools and techniques to treat conditions that affect the spine. Learn more about what to expect with MISS.

MISS involves using specialized tools and techniques to treat conditions that affect the spine. Learn more about what to expect with MISS.

### Before

### During

### After

### Before MISS

Our team thoroughly explains the procedure and risks and answers any questions you have. Before surgery, you undergo a series of tests, including spine imaging. You may also have blood tests and an electrocardiogram to ensure you are healthy enough for surgery. You’ll receive detailed instructions to prepare you for surgery, such as:

### During MISS

You receive anesthesia so that you don’t feel pain during surgery. We typically use general anesthesia, which allows you to sleep during the procedure.

In some cases, spine surgery under local anesthesia, also known as awake spine surgery, is performed, offering many benefits. At this time, local anesthesia is only offered by [Dr. Todd Alamin](https://stanfordhealthcare.org/doctors/a/todd-alamin.html).

Awake surgery has many benefits, including:

There are many types of MISS procedures. Your surgeon may access your spine through small incisions in your back, chest, abdomen, or side. Intraoperative imaging helps guide the procedure. We may also perform the surgery using a robotic platform or similar state-of-the-art surgical system. These advanced tools allow for superior precision and safety.

### After MISS

After the procedure, we close your incisions with stitches or glue and cover them with surgical tape or bandages. We transfer you to a postsurgical area for observation. Depending on the type of surgery, you may go home that day or spend several days in the hospital.  
  
Complete recovery takes time. Your surgeon will tell you what to expect and what follow-up care you need, such as physical therapy. Our team is here for you if you have any problems or questions.

*   Minimally Invasive Surgery

Minimally Invasive Surgery
==========================

Everything You Need To Know About Minimally Invasive Surgery

Request More Information

Overview
--------

Minimally invasive surgery is a surgical approach that uses small incisions, usually less than two inches in length, to perform surgical procedures. This type of surgery is ideal for patients with specific health problems who would otherwise not be able to undergo the recovery process required by traditional open surgery due to the nature of their condition or injury. Minimally invasive surgery is also helpful in treating cancer and chronic pain conditions such as arthritis. While it does require more recovery time than traditional open surgery, it is often much less painful for patients. It allows doctors to operate on larger areas of skin without causing scarring or leaving large incisions behind after the procedure is complete.

How It Works
------------

Minimally invasive surgery is a surgical approach that uses smaller incisions and less invasive equipment than traditional open surgery. The goal of minimally invasive surgery is to provide patients with faster recovery times, fewer complications, and better quality of life following surgery. Minimally invasive surgery is a surgical procedure that uses a small incision to gain access to the body. This surgery method may be used for back surgery, knee surgery, or other medical procedures.

Minimally invasive surgery involves using smaller incisions, shorter operating times, and less invasive tools to achieve the same results as traditional open surgery. Instead of making large incisions in the body and performing complicated procedures on the inside, minimally invasive medical procedures use smaller and simpler tools that allow doctors to operate on specific areas of the body with less trauma.

Minimally invasive surgery can be performed on either internal or external body parts. Internal surgeries include:

*   Hysterectomy.
*   Prostatectomy (surgical removal of the prostate gland).
*   Colonoscopy (examination of the entire gastrointestinal tract).
*   Other procedures involve small incisions in internal organs.
*   External surgeries involve removing tumors or damaged tissue from areas near major blood vessels or lymph nodes while leaving healthy tissue intact around them.

Conditions and Symptoms Treated
-------------------------------

Minimally invasive surgery is a surgical procedure that uses minimally invasive techniques to perform surgery. Minimally invasive procedures are performed under local anesthesia, which means that patients are given local anesthesia in the area of the operation, and they can still feel pain. Minimally invasive procedures include laparoscopic surgery, laparoscopy, percutaneous gastrostomy, endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography, and endoscopic ultrasound.

Minimally invasive surgery (MIS) is a medical procedure category that uses small incisions and instruments to perform surgeries. MIS procedures can be performed through an open procedure (known as an open procedure) or a minimally invasive closed procedure.

In minimally invasive surgery, an incision is made in the skin to make it easier for doctors and nurses to see inside the body. The doctor or nurse who performs the procedure will insert a camera through this incision and use it to view what is inside your body. They may use special instruments to remove damaged tissue or perform other procedures on your body.

Minimally invasive surgeries are often used for common conditions such as:

*   Arthritis
*   Breathing problems
*   Chronic pain
*   Diabetes complications
*   Epilepsy
*   Hernia repair

Success Rate and Results
------------------------

The success rate of minimally invasive surgery for prostate cancer is about 95%, and results are generally better than those with open surgery.

Minimally invasive surgery is a minimally invasive surgery that can be performed using a tube (or catheter) instead of incisions. In some cases, surgeons may use a tube to insert instruments into the prostate gland without making any incisions on the body.

**You must meet one of these two criteria:** You cannot perform open radical prostatectomy (RP), which means it is not possible to remove your entire prostate gland at once because it would damage nearby structures, or you cannot perform robotic-assisted RP due to blood loss, nerve damage, or another health problem requiring immediate intervention.

The Benefit of Minimally Invasive Surgery
-----------------------------------------

Minimally invasive surgery (MIS) is the newest addition to the medical field and has been used for a considerable time. The benefit of minimally invasive surgery is that it allows the patient to be awake and alert during the operation, giving them more control over their recovery process. Minimally invasive surgery is a growing part of the medical field, as it has become increasingly popular among surgeons. It is a method of treating patients with laparoscopic surgery and other minimally invasive surgery.

Minimally invasive surgery can be performed on patients who have had problems with previous surgeries, including those who have had complications from traditional open surgeries. Minimal-invasive surgery helps reduce pain and recovery time for patients while improving their quality of life.

**The benefits of minimally invasive surgery include the following:**

*   The contrast agent may be injected into a vein, so x-rays can be taken on a film after the contrast agent has been injected into an artery to make an image of a blood vessel or other internal organ.
*   Injections of medicines may be given directly into arteries to reduce swelling or inflammation caused by other treatments.
*   Minimally invasive surgery is a type of surgery that uses a small incision to perform a procedure. The benefit of this type of surgery is that it can provide patients with fewer complications and shorter recovery times.
*   Surgical instruments can be inserted through tiny incisions without cutting through skin or muscle. This means no scars and faster healing times than with traditional surgery.

Risk of this Treatment
----------------------

Minimally invasive surgeries are typically done through several small incisions instead of one large. This allows the surgeon to get closer to the area they need to work on while also making it easier for them to see what they are doing. There are some exceptions, however, such as breast reduction or liposuction, where it is necessary for the surgeon to enter the body through a larger incision so that they can reach certain parts of the body.

The risks associated with minimally invasive surgery are similar to those of traditional open surgery. However, some studies have shown that minimally invasive procedures may be associated with a lower risk of complications than those associated with open surgeries. The risk of this treatment is low. It is a minor surgery that usually does not require any stitches. The downside is that you may undergo this procedure several times to get rid of your cancer.

Recovery
--------

Recovery time depends on your situation, but most patients can return to work within two weeks of surgery. Most people can return to normal activities within a few weeks. If you have a job requiring you to be on your feet for long periods, it is best to wait until your stitches heal before resuming full-time work.

Recovery from minimally invasive surgery is generally faster than traditional open surgeries and less painful. In addition, you will have fewer complications with minimally invasive surgery. Recovery from minimally invasive surgery takes longer than open surgery but is still relatively short. Most patients can go home on the same day as their operation. After an outpatient procedure, most people have a follow-up appointment with their surgeon within one week. If you have any complications, you will be seen at another hospital.

Before, During, and After the Treatment
---------------------------------------

**Before the treatment**

Before you get your procedure, we will send you a letter explaining what to expect. You will meet with our team of surgeons, who will explain your options and answer any questions you have. We will also discuss the risks and benefits of each procedure.

Please ask us any questions you might have before your surgery date. We are happy to answer them before or after your appointment so that you can make an informed decision about the best option for you.

**During your treatment**

During your minimally invasive surgery, you will lie on an operating table as one of our surgeons performs the procedure. They use ultrasound technology to guide them through complex procedures while minimizing pain when inserting or removing implants and other surgical devices.

**After Surgery**

After surgery, They checked on my blood pressure periodically throughout the night and morning, but it never rose above normal levels (which is perfect). A minimally invasive procedure involves making smaller incisions than traditional open surgeries, which can reduce the risk of infection or bleeding. Minimally invasive surgery may also be used for spinal fusion, carpal tunnel release, and cataract removal.

Why choose our physicians?
--------------------------

Our physicians are board certified in general surgery, plastic surgery, and reconstructive surgery. They have been practicing at the same location for more than many years. Our physicians have made a name for themselves by providing patients with the highest quality of care worldwide.

Our physicians are dedicated to providing the most advanced surgical procedures available to you with minimal recovery time and discomfort. Our surgeons perform minimally invasive surgeries using robotic equipment and techniques that offer a more natural healing process without damage or scarring.

Our physicians are highly trained in the latest techniques and procedures, focusing on minimally invasive surgery. They will work closely with you to determine which treatment is best for you.

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------

[### Is it safe?](https://larkinhealth.com/en/treatments-services/orthopedics/minimally-invasive-surgery/#accordion-07-body-01)

The minimally invasive surgery is safe for most people with no significant side effects. However, it does not always work perfectly for all patients, so there may be some risks involved with this type of surgery.

[### How much time will it take to recover?](https://larkinhealth.com/en/treatments-services/orthopedics/minimally-invasive-surgery/#accordion-07-body-01)

Recovery time after minimally invasive surgery is usually less than that of open surgery. Most people can generally recover from minimally invasive surgery within two weeks. Minor complications are possible with any surgery, but they are more likely to happen with minimally invasive surgery.

[### How Much Cost Will be Required for the Minimally Invasive Surgery?](https://larkinhealth.com/en/treatments-services/orthopedics/minimally-invasive-surgery/#accordion-07-body-01)

The cost of minimally invasive surgery varies depending on where it is performed and what procedure it involves. In general, though, the cost will be higher than traditional open surgeries because the equipment used during minimally invasive procedures costs more per unit than traditional open surgeries.

[### Do You Need to Hospitalize for Minimally Invasive Surgery?](https://larkinhealth.com/en/treatments-services/orthopedics/minimally-invasive-surgery/#accordion-07-body-01)

Minimally invasive surgery is a safe procedure. However, it does require some precautions and follow-up care. Patients who undergo minimally invasive surgery must be under close observation for two weeks after the operation. This means they will have to stay in the hospital until they are no longer at risk of infection or bleeding.

# Minimally Invasive Surgery FAQ

### 

Minimally invasive surgery involves performing procedures through a series of small (less than a half-inch) incisions instead of a single large incision, which can be up to 24 inches long, depending on the type of surgery.

Procedures are performed with a combination of thin tubes with cameras, surgical instruments and retractors, making this approach possible. In robot-assisted procedures, the surgeon manipulates these thin tubes with robotic arms that perform procedures with precision and no risk of tremor.

The advantages of minimally invasive surgery include less postoperative pain, faster recovery and restoration and reduced incisional complications compared to traditional open surgery. For surgeons, the minimally invasive approach allows for improved visibility due to better lighting and magnification with the cameras. Since each person operating can visualize the area of interest equally, there is potentially greater safety with this approach than with open surgery.

There are very few additional risks to minimally invasive surgery. Most patients tolerate minimally invasive procedures far better than they would traditional open surgical procedures. It is important to discuss health issues with your physician prior to undergoing surgery of any kind.

Most surgical procedures can be performed with a minimally invasive approach. This involves operations removing or reconstructing the esophagus, stomach, gall bladder, liver, spleen, pancreas, small and large intestine, appendix, adrenal glands, kidneys, bladder, and male and female genitourinary organs.

Single laparoscopic incision transabdominal surgery (SLITS) and Natural Orifice Translumenal Endoscopic Surgery (NOTES) are areas of active research in Minimally Invasive Surgery. SLITS allows for minimally invasive procedures to be completed through a single site on the abdominal wall further reducing the incisional morbidity associated with surgery. In some cases, the incision is buried in the umbilicus and is invisible after the operation. Currently, SLITS is used for operations involving gall bladder removal, gastric banding, sleeve gastrectomy, and appendectomy.

For more information or to schedule a consultation, please call 714-456-8008.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Minimally invasive surgery may not be appropriate for complex conditions or patients with specific anatomical challenges, requiring a thorough evaluation by surgeons.
Despite being safer than traditional methods, risks such as infection, bleeding, and anesthesia reactions remain, similar to those seen in open surgeries.
Surgeons require specialized training and practice to master minimally invasive techniques, which can lead to variability in outcomes and patient experiences.
The advanced technology and equipment needed for minimally invasive procedures can result in higher upfront costs compared to traditional surgery.
Minimally invasive surgeries rely on sophisticated tools and imaging systems, necessitating access to state-of-the-art facilities that may not be universally available.
